title: Peking Duck
description: Peking Duck is a legendary dish boasting incredibly crispy, lacquered skin and succulent, flavorful meat. Served with delicate pancakes, fresh scallions, crisp cucumber, and rich hoisin sauce, it's a true culinary masterpiece cherished for its exquisite textures and harmonious flavors.

ingredients.notes: Essential for crispy skin and color

instructions.title: Prepare the Duck for Glazing
instructions.text: Thoroughly rinse the duck inside and out with cold water. Pat it completely dry with paper towels. Remove any excess fat from the cavity and around the neck. Using a sharp knife or kitchen shears, remove the wingtips and the first joint of the wings. Prick the skin all over with a fork, especially in fatty areas like the breast and thighs, without piercing the meat. This helps fat render and skin crisp.

instructions.title: Blanch and Glaze the Duck
instructions.text: Bring a large pot of water (about 8-10 cups) to a rolling boil. Place the duck on a rack over a sink or a large roasting pan. Carefully pour the boiling water all over the duck's skin, ensuring every part is scalded. This tightens the skin. Immediately pat dry again. In a bowl, whisk together the maltose syrup, Shaoxing wine, Chinese red vinegar, dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, and salt to create the glaze. Generously brush the glaze all over the duck, ensuring an even coating.

instructions.title: Air Dry for Crispy Skin
instructions.text: This is the most crucial step for achieving crispy skin. Insert a hook or a string through the duck's neck, or place it on a rack in a cool, well-ventilated area, preferably in front of a fan. Alternatively, hang it in a refrigerator (ensure no strong odors are present) for a minimum of 12 hours, and ideally 24-36 hours. The skin must be completely dry to the touch, resembling parchment paper. This prolonged drying renders the fat and creates the iconic crispy skin.

instructions.title: Preheat Oven and Prepare for Roasting
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C). Fill a small bowl with hot water and place it on the bottom rack of the oven; this creates steam to help keep the meat moist. Place the dried duck, breast-side up, on a roasting rack set inside a roasting pan. Add about 1-2 cups of water to the bottom of the roasting pan to catch drippings and prevent burning. This also adds moisture to the oven.

instructions.title: Roast the Duck
instructions.text: Roast the duck for 1 hour at 375°F (190°C). Then, reduce the oven temperature to 325°F (160°C) and continue roasting for another 1 to 1.5 hours, or until an instant-read thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the thigh registers 165°F (74°C). During the last 30 minutes, you can increase the temperature back to 400°F (200°C) if the skin isn't as crisp as desired, watching carefully to prevent burning. The skin should be deep golden brown and exceptionally crispy.

instructions.title: Rest, Carve, and Serve
instructions.text: Once roasted, remove the duck from the oven and let it rest on a cutting board, loosely tented with foil, for at least 15-20 minutes. This allows the juices to redistribute, ensuring tender meat. While the duck rests, warm the mandarin pancakes according to package instructions, and arrange the julienned scallions and cucumber on a serving platter. To carve, carefully slice off the crispy skin in small pieces, then thinly slice the meat. Serve immediately with warm pancakes, hoisin sauce, scallions, and cucumber, allowing each diner to assemble their own wrap.

tips: **The Air Drying is Non-Negotiable:** Do not skip or shorten the air-drying process. It is the absolute key to achieving the signature crispy skin.

**Maltose Syrup for Shine:** Maltose syrup gives the duck its beautiful lacquered finish and helps in achieving a wonderfully crisp texture. Do not substitute with regular sugar or honey if you want authentic results.
**Proper Carving Technique:** For the traditional experience, carve the crispy skin separately first, then slice the succulent meat thinly. This allows diners to enjoy both textures distinctly.
**Steam for Moisture:** The bowl of hot water in the oven helps maintain a moist environment, preventing the meat from drying out while the skin crisps.
article:
## History of Peking Duck

Peking Duck, or Běijīng Kǎoyā, is a dish with a rich imperial history dating back to the Yuan Dynasty in the 13th century. It first appeared in culinary records during the Ming Dynasty, becoming a favorite of the imperial court. By the Qing Dynasty, it was a staple of royal banquets and a symbol of culinary prestige. The technique of roasting ducks in closed ovens (焖炉, mēnlú) was perfected, followed later by the open-oven (挂炉, guàlú) method, which is more common today. Over centuries, Peking Duck evolved from a court delicacy to a globally recognized symbol of Chinese cuisine, celebrated for its unique preparation and unforgettable taste.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe rigorously adheres to traditional Chinese techniques, ensuring an authentic Peking Duck experience. The crucial element is the prolonged air-drying process, which dehydrates the skin, allowing it to become incredibly crisp and brittle during roasting. The maltose-based glaze is vital; it caramelizes beautifully, creating the deep, lustrous color and contributing significantly to the skin's crackling texture and subtle sweetness. The initial blanching tightens the skin, further aiding in crispness. Roasting at precise temperatures ensures the fat renders fully while the meat remains succulent. This meticulous combination of steps guarantees the iconic contrast of crisp skin and tender meat that defines true Peking Duck.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To elevate your Peking Duck, start with a high-quality, fresh Pekin duck, as its fat content is ideal. Ensure every inch of the duck is thoroughly dried after blanching and before glazing. When air-drying, a fan significantly speeds up the process and ensures even drying. If using a refrigerator, place the duck on a rack over a tray to catch any drips, and ensure it's uncovered. During roasting, resist the urge to open the oven frequently, as this drops the temperature and extends cooking time. When carving, use a very sharp knife to achieve thin, elegant slices of skin and meat. Practice makes perfect for carving, so don't be discouraged if your first attempt isn't flawless.

## Serving Suggestions

Peking Duck is traditionally served in a specific manner to fully appreciate its complex textures and flavors. The crispy skin and tender meat are carved and presented alongside thin, steamed Mandarin pancakes (荷叶饼, héyèbǐng). Accompanying condiments typically include julienned fresh spring onions (scallions) and crisp cucumber sticks, which provide a refreshing counterpoint to the rich duck. A rich, savory hoisin sauce (海鲜酱, hǎixiānjiàng) is essential for spreading on the pancakes before adding the duck, scallions, and cucumber. Each diner then wraps these components into a delightful, savory-sweet parcel, creating a balanced and interactive culinary experience.

Served with delicate pancakes, fresh scallions, crisp cucumber, and rich hoisin sauce, it's a true culinary masterpiece cherished for its exquisite textures and harmonious flavors.
jsonLd.keywords: Chinese, Chinese, authentic, traditional, dinner, Chinese, Peking Duck, authentic, traditional, dinner, roast duck, crispy skin
recipeIngredient: 1 (4-5 lb) Whole Pekin Duck (Preferably fresh, not frozen)
1/2 cup Maltose Syrup (Essential for crispy skin and color)
2 tbsp Chinese Red Vinegar (Or good quality rice vinegar)
